pages:
Sources: American Cancer Society, Inc: Cancer.org
Breast cancer: what you need to know 
Breast cancer develops from cells in the breast. The most common 
sign of breast cancer is a new lump or mass (though it’s important to 
know that, most of the time, breast lumps and masses are benign). 
Other signs include a generalized swelling of part of a breast (even if 
no lump is felt), skin irritation or dimpling, nipple pain or retraction, 
redness or scaliness of the nipple or breast skin, or a spontaneous 
discharge other than breast milk. 
Reducing your risk
We don’t know how to prevent 
breast cancer, but it’s possible for 
most women to reduce their risk of 
developing the disease. For instance, 
certain lifestyle choices are associated 
with a lower risk of breast cancer, such 
as reducing alcohol use, breastfeeding, 
engaging in regular physical activity 
and staying at a healthy weight. If  
a woman is at higher risk of breast 
cancer, she may be able to reduce her 
likelihood of developing the disease  by taking estrogen-blocking drugs,  
such as tamoxifen and raloxifene.  
There are also some risk factors 
that can’t be changed, such as age, 
race, family history of disease and 
reproductive history. 
Finding it early
The earlier breast cancer is found, 
the better the chances for successful 
treatment. A mammogram can often 
help a doctor detect breast cancer 
before physical symptoms develop. Getting the treatment you need
Treatment is most successful when 
breast cancer is detected early. 
Depending on the situation and the 
patient’s choices, treatment may 
involve breast conservation surgery (like 
lumpectomy) or mastectomy. In both 
cases, the surgeon may also remove 
lymph nodes under the arm. Women 
who have a mastectomy have several 
options for breast reconstruction. 
Other treatments are radiation therapy, 
chemotherapy, hormone therapy and 
monoclonal antibody therapy. Often, 
two or more methods are used in 
combination. Patients should discuss all 
treatment options with their doctors.

w434662a_9-20Sources: American Cancer Society, Inc: Cancer.org
cancer.org   |   1.800.227.2345Who is at risk? 
Women
Being a woman is the greatest risk 
factor for breast cancer. However,  
men can develop breast cancer, too.  
… who are older
The risk of developing breast cancer 
increases with age. Most kinds of 
invasive breast cancers are primarily 
found in women 50 and older. 
… or who have a family history 
of the disease
The risk of breast cancer is higher 
among women with a family history 
of the disease. If a woman has a first-
degree relative with breast cancer, her 
own risk of the disease is higher—and 
if she has more than one first-degree 
relative who has or had breast cancer 
before the age of 40 or in both breasts, 
her own risk increases even more. 
However, most women with breast 
cancer don’t have a first-degree relative 
with the disease. 
Other risk factors
 –Taking post-menopausal hormone 
therapy with estrogen and 
progesterone 
 –Being overweight or obese, especially 
after menopause
 –Using alcohol 
 –Being physically inactive
 –Having a long menstrual history 
(starting to menstruate early or going 
into menopause late, or both)
 –Never having children or having a first 
live birth after age 30
 –Having a history of breast cancer or 
certain benign breast conditions Quality of life  
All cancer patients and survivors have 
their quality of life affected in some 
way, such as socially, psychologically, 
physically and spiritually. 
Some of the concerns that patients 
and survivors most often express are 
fear of recurrence, chronic and/or acute 
pain, sexual problems, fatigue, guilt for 
delaying care or for doing things that 
may have caused the cancer, changes in 
physical appearance, depression, sleep 
difficulties, changes in what they are 
able to do after treatment, and the 
burden caused by cancer on finances 
and loved ones. Women with breast 
cancer also often feel uncertainty 
about treatment options. 
Bottom line
Regular mammograms can help  
find breast cancer at an early stage, 
when treatment is most successful.  
A mammogram can find breast  
changes that could be cancer years 
before physical symptoms develop. 
Some things that may help reduce  
a woman’s risk of getting breast  
cancer include being physically  
active, staying at a healthy weight  
and limiting alcohol use. Breast cancer by the numbers*  
 –New cases:
• Invasive breast cancer  
in women: 276,480
• Carcinoma in situ (CIS)  
in women: 48,530
• Invasive breast cancer  
in men: 2,620
 –Deaths:
• Women: 42,170
• Men: 520
 –Five-year relative survival rate for 
localized stages: 99%
 –Five-year relative survival rate for all 
stages combined: 90%
*2020 U.S. estimates
